question: What device priority does resolve_device() use when device is "auto"?
answer: According to the code in `test_auto_falls_back_to_cpu` and `test_default_argument_is_auto`, when device is set as "auto", if neither CUDA nor Apple MPS are available, resolve_device() will use CPU. The function test_auto_falls_back_to_cpu sets the conditions where both torch.cuda (CUDA) and torch.backends.mps (Apple MPS) availability is false using monkeypatching techniques. In this scenario, it asserts that resolve_device("auto") returns "cpu". Similarly, test_default_argument_is_auto also sets the same conditions without specifying a device and checks if resolve_device() defaults to CPU when no CUDA or Apple MPS is available by setting monkeypatch.setattr(torch.cuda, "is_available", lambda: False) and monkeypatch.setattr(torch.backends.mps, "is_available", lambda: False). The assertion in this test confirms that resolve_device() indeed defaults to CPU when the device is set as auto under these conditions.

question: Why was the M1 agent loop rewritten from a fixed search-only pipeline into a tool-choosing loop?
answer:
The M1 agent loop was rewritten from a fixed search-only pipeline into a tool-choosing loop so that the LLM itself can decide which tool to call and take multiple steps (e.g., search, read a file, then search again) instead of being restricted to one fixed search action per attempt. This change allows for more flexibility and mirroring how real coding agents navigate a codebase.
